tools.name: configuration_view
tools.description: Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML

minified.description: Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)

tools.name: events_list
tools.description: List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter events by field values (e.g. 'type=Warning', 'involvedObject.name=my-pod'). Supported fields: involvedObject.kind, involvedObject.name, involvedObject.namespace, involvedObject.uid, involvedObject.apiVersion, involvedObject.resourceVersion, involvedObject.fieldPath, reason, reportingComponent, source, type. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces

tools.name: namespaces_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter namespaces by field values (e.g. 'metadata.name=default', 'status.phase=Active'). Supported fields: metadata.name, status.phase. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

tools.name: nodes_log
tools.description: Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet

name.description: Name of the node to get logs from

query.description: query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")

tailLines.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)

tools.name: nodes_stats_summary
tools.description: Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics

name.description: Name of the node to get stats from

tools.name: nodes_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)

tools.name: pods_delete
tools.description: Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

name.description: Name of the Pod to delete

namespace.description: Namespace to delete the Pod from

tools.name: pods_exec
tools.description: Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command

command.description: Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]

container.description: Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod where the command will be executed

namespace.description: Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed

tools.name: pods_get
tools.description: Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod from

tools.name: pods_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_list_in_namespace
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_log
tools.description: Get the logs of a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

container.description: Name of the Pod container to get the logs from (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the logs from

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod logs from

tail.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, default: 100)

tools.name: pods_run
tools.description: Run a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided container image and optional name

image.description: Container Image to run in the Pod

name.description: Name of the Pod (Optional, random name if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to run the Pod in

port.description: TCP/IP port to expose from the Pod container (Optional, no port exposed if not provided)

tools.name: pods_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Pods in the all namespaces, the provided namespace, or the current namespace

all_namespaces.description: If true, list the resource consumption for all Pods in all namespaces. If false, list the resource consumption for Pods in the provided namespace or the current namespace

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Pods in the namespace if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pods resource consumption from (Optional, current namespace if not provided and all_namespaces is false)

tools.name: resources_create_or_update
tools.description:
Create or update a Kubernetes resource via Server-Side Apply. The manifest is the complete desired state: any field this tool previously set and the new manifest omits is removed. To edit an existing resource, fetch it with resources_get, modify it, then re-apply the full resource.
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

resource.description: Complete YAML or JSON representation of the Kubernetes resource (full desired state, not a partial patch). Include apiVersion, kind, metadata, and the full spec.

tools.name: resources_delete
tools.description:
Delete a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

gracePeriodSeconds.description: Optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to delete the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will delete resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_get
tools.description:
Get a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_list
tools.description:
List Kubernetes resources and objects in the current cluster by providing their apiVersion and kind and optionally the namespace and label selector
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resources (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter resources by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'metadata.name=myresource'). Supported fields vary by resource type. For Pods: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

kind.description: kind of the resources (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the resources by label

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resources from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will list resources from all namespaces

tools.name: resources_scale
tools.description: Get or update the scale of a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally the namespace. If the scale is set in the tool call, the scale will be updated to that value. Always returns the current scale of the resource

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are apps/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: StatefulSet, Deployment)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to get/update the namespaced resource scale from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get/update resource scale from configured namespace

scale.description: Optional scale to update the resources scale to. If not provided, will return the current scale of the resource, and not update it

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I did
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace canary-deployment-ns) with 2 replicas, image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29, container name rec-engine, containerPort 80, and the same (empty) pod securityContext as the stable deployment. Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app=recommendation-engine (removed version filter) so it targets both engine-v2-0 and engine-v2-1. Service port remains 80 -> targetPort 8080.

Verification
- Pods:
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- Endpoints for recommendation-engine now include 4 addresses (2 from v2.0, 2 from v2.1) on port 8080, so traffic will be roughly 50/50.

Notes and next steps
- To adjust the traffic split, scale replicas (traffic approximately follows endpoint count):
  - Example: 3 stable, 1 canary ≈ 75/25; 2 stable, 1 canary ≈ 67/33.
- To roll back canary routing quickly, either:
  - Update the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0, or
  - Scale engine-v2-1 to 0 replicas.

Steps.message:
namespace/canary-deployment-ns created
deployment.apps/engine-v2-0 created
service/recommendation-engine created

Done.

What I did
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace canary-deployment-ns) with 2 replicas, image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29, container name rec-engine, containerPort 80, and the same (empty) pod securityContext as the stable deployment. Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app=recommendation-engine (removed version filter) so it targets both engine-v2-0 and engine-v2-1. Service port remains 80 -> targetPort 8080.

Verification
- Pods:
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- Endpoints for recommendation-engine now include 4 addresses (2 from v2.0, 2 from v2.1) on port 8080, so traffic will be roughly 50/50.

Notes and next steps
- To adjust the traffic split, scale replicas (traffic approximately follows endpoint count):
  - Example: 3 stable, 1 canary ≈ 75/25; 2 stable, 1 canary ≈ 67/33.
- To roll back canary routing quickly, either:
  - Update the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0, or
  - Scale engine-v2-1 to 0 replicas.

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I did
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace canary-deployment-ns) with 2 replicas, image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29, container name rec-engine, containerPort 80, and the same (empty) pod securityContext as the stable deployment. Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app=recommendation-engine (removed version filter) so it targets both engine-v2-0 and engine-v2-1. Service port remains 80 -> targetPort 8080.

Verification
- Pods:
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- Endpoints for recommendation-engine now include 4 addresses (2 from v2.0, 2 from v2.1) on port 8080, so traffic will be roughly 50/50.

Notes and next steps
- To adjust the traffic split, scale replicas (traffic approximately follows endpoint count):
  - Example: 3 stable, 1 canary ≈ 75/25; 2 stable, 1 canary ≈ 67/33.
- To roll back canary routing quickly, either:
  - Update the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0, or
  - Scale engine-v2-1 to 0 replicas.

deployment.apps/engine-v2-0 condition met

Canary deployment is correctly configured and receiving traffic.

import time

print("Starting calc-app...")

            sys.stdout.flush()

    sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
    time.sleep(sleep_time)

data:
  calc-app.py: |
    import random
    import sys
    import time

    print("Starting calc-app...")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    counter = 0
    while True:
        counter += 1
        if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
            try:
                result = 1 / 0
            except ZeroDivisionError as e:
                print(f"Run {counter} failed with error: {e}")
                sys.stdout.flush()
        else:
            print(f"Run {counter} succeeded")
            sys.stdout.flush()

        sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
        time.sleep(sleep_time)

print("Starting calc-app...")
sys.stdout.flush()

counter = 0
while True:

    if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:

outputSteps.content:
Short answer: nothing is wrong with the Pod from Kubernetes’ perspective.

- Pod status: Running and Ready, 0 restarts. Events are all Normal (Scheduled, Pulled, Started).
- Logs show intermittent “division by zero” messages, but that behavior comes directly from your app code in the calc-app-map ConfigMap. The script intentionally does 1/0 in a try/except on some runs and just prints the error, so the container keeps running.

If you don’t want those “failed” lines:
- Remove the forced 1/0 block or change it to a safe operation (for example, result = 1 / random.randint(1, 4)).
- Or just log a “skipped” run instead of raising an error.

Note: your sleep_time grows exponentially (1.2**counter / 20), so the app will slow down a lot over time. Consider capping or using a fixed/backoff with a max to avoid very long sleeps.

Steps.message: Pass under CONTAINS: The model response explicitly includes the core information from the reference, namely “division by zero.” It states that logs show intermittent “division by zero” messages and explains that the app intentionally does 1/0. Extra contextual details do not contradict the reference and are permitted. Therefore, it semantically contains the reference’s content.

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed the CrashLoopBackOff in app (namespace crashloop-test).

What was wrong
- The container was started with: command: ["/bin/sh","-c"] and args: ["nonexistent_command"], which fails. Logs showed: /bin/sh: 1: nonexistent_command: not found.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to start nginx in the foreground:
  - command: ["nginx"]
  - args: ["-g", "daemon off;"]
  (Alternatively, we could omit command/args entirely and use the image’s default entrypoint.)

Current status
- New pod: app-56b4d5b87f-cl9qr is Running (1/1).
- Deployment app is Available with 1/1 ready replica and has successfully progressed.
- The old crashing pod from the previous ReplicaSet shows Error and will be cleaned up automatically.

If you want, I can:
- Remove the explicit command/args to rely on the image default.
- Add readiness/liveness probes for nginx.
- Scale replicas or wire up a Service/Ingress.

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed the CrashLoopBackOff in app (namespace crashloop-test).

What was wrong
- The container was started with: command: ["/bin/sh","-c"] and args: ["nonexistent_command"], which fails. Logs showed: /bin/sh: 1: nonexistent_command: not found.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to start nginx in the foreground:
  - command: ["nginx"]
  - args: ["-g", "daemon off;"]
  (Alternatively, we could omit command/args entirely and use the image’s default entrypoint.)

Current status
- New pod: app-56b4d5b87f-cl9qr is Running (1/1).
- Deployment app is Available with 1/1 ready replica and has successfully progressed.
- The old crashing pod from the previous ReplicaSet shows Error and will be cleaned up automatically.

If you want, I can:
- Remove the explicit command/args to rely on the image default.
- Add readiness/liveness probes for nginx.
- Scale replicas or wire up a Service/Ingress.

results.taskOutput:
Done. Here’s what I fixed and the current status:

- Root cause: The Pod was in ImagePullBackOff due to an invalid image tag: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag.
- Fix: Updated Deployment debug/app to use a valid image: nginx:1.27-alpine.
- Result: Rollout completed successfully.
  - Deployment status: Available=True, 1/1 ready.
  - New Pod: app-696ff4db6-kmnrx is Running (1/1).

If you prefer the unprivileged NGINX image from Quay, tell me the exact tag you want (e.g., quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:<valid-tag>) and I can switch it.

outputSteps.content:
Done. Here’s what I fixed and the current status:

- Root cause: The Pod was in ImagePullBackOff due to an invalid image tag: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag.
- Fix: Updated Deployment debug/app to use a valid image: nginx:1.27-alpine.
- Result: Rollout completed successfully.
  - Deployment status: Available=True, 1/1 ready.
  - New Pod: app-696ff4db6-kmnrx is Running (1/1).

If you prefer the unprivileged NGINX image from Quay, tell me the exact tag you want (e.g., quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:<valid-tag>) and I can switch it.

deployment.apps/app condition met
Pod is stable. Verification successful.

results.taskOutput:
Done. Your pod is now Running.

What I fixed
- Root cause: The PVC homepage-pvc referenced a non-existent StorageClass "sc", so the pod couldn’t be scheduled (event: “pod has unbound immediate PersistentVolumeClaims”).
- Action taken:
  - Deleted the pending PVC homepage-pvc (no data loss since it was never bound).
  - Recreated it with a valid StorageClass: standard (rancher.io/local-path).
- Result:
  - PVC homepage-pvc is Bound (1Gi).
  - Pod homepage-pod is Running on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane, Ready, IP 10.244.0.29.

Notes
- If you intended to use a different StorageClass, tell me which one and I’ll switch the claim accordingly.
- For future manifests, either omit storageClassName to use the default, or ensure the specified class exists (e.g., standard or csi-hostpath-sc in this cluster).

outputSteps.content:
Done. Your pod is now Running.

What I fixed
- Root cause: The PVC homepage-pvc referenced a non-existent StorageClass "sc", so the pod couldn’t be scheduled (event: “pod has unbound immediate PersistentVolumeClaims”).
- Action taken:
  - Deleted the pending PVC homepage-pvc (no data loss since it was never bound).
  - Recreated it with a valid StorageClass: standard (rancher.io/local-path).
- Result:
  - PVC homepage-pvc is Bound (1Gi).
  - Pod homepage-pod is Running on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane, Ready, IP 10.244.0.29.

Notes
- If you intended to use a different StorageClass, tell me which one and I’ll switch the claim accordingly.
- For future manifests, either omit storageClassName to use the default, or ensure the specified class exists (e.g., standard or csi-hostpath-sc in this cluster).

Steps.message:

ℹWaiting for PVC 'homepage-pvc' to be 'Bound'...
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc condition met
'homepage-pvc' is Bound. Verifying that desired state is realized...
Waiting for Pod 'homepage-pod' to be 'Ready'...
pod/homepage-pod condition met
Pod 'homepage-pod' is Ready. Verification successful for .

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the nginx unprivileged image (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged), which listens on port 8080 by default.
- Probes and the Service were pointing to port 80, so health checks failed and the Service had no ready endpoints.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to use readiness and liveness probes against path / on port 8080.
- Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated the Service to keep service port 80 but targetPort 8080 (so clients still use 80).

Current status
- Pod is healthy and Ready.
- Service endpoints are populated and routing to the pod.

How to verify
- kubectl -n orders get pods
- kubectl -n orders get svc webapp
- kubectl -n orders get endpoints webapp
- Optional quick test from your machine: kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access (Ingress/LoadBalancer/NodePort), tell me your preference and hostname, and I’ll set it up.

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the nginx unprivileged image (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged), which listens on port 8080 by default.
- Probes and the Service were pointing to port 80, so health checks failed and the Service had no ready endpoints.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to use readiness and liveness probes against path / on port 8080.
- Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated the Service to keep service port 80 but targetPort 8080 (so clients still use 80).

Current status
- Pod is healthy and Ready.
- Service endpoints are populated and routing to the pod.

How to verify
- kubectl -n orders get pods
- kubectl -n orders get svc webapp
- kubectl -n orders get endpoints webapp
- Optional quick test from your machine: kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access (Ingress/LoadBalancer/NodePort), tell me your preference and hostname, and I’ll set it up.

toolCalls.title: resources_create_or_update

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 2
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 2
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 2
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  selector:
    app: webapp
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
    protocol: TCP
  type: ClusterIP

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the nginx unprivileged image (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged), which listens on port 8080 by default.
- Probes and the Service were pointing to port 80, so health checks failed and the Service had no ready endpoints.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to use readiness and liveness probes against path / on port 8080.
- Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated the Service to keep service port 80 but targetPort 8080 (so clients still use 80).

Current status
- Pod is healthy and Ready.
- Service endpoints are populated and routing to the pod.

How to verify
- kubectl -n orders get pods
- kubectl -n orders get svc webapp
- kubectl -n orders get endpoints webapp
- Optional quick test from your machine: kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access (Ingress/LoadBalancer/NodePort), tell me your preference and hostname, and I’ll set it up.

Steps.message:
Checking if the pod is running and ready...
pod/webapp-79cfdb4f44-fmh67 condition met
Success: Pod is now Ready
Current liveness probe path: /
Current readiness probe path: /
Success: Both probe paths have been fixed
Success: Pod is stable with acceptable number of restarts

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing deployments in the apps API group, not pods. So the pod-reader ServiceAccount had no permission to list pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to grant pod access:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

Your binding already correctly targets ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader, so no change was needed there.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i list pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

If you need to list pods cluster-wide, we can switch to a ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding.

outputSteps.content:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing deployments in the apps API group, not pods. So the pod-reader ServiceAccount had no permission to list pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to grant pod access:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

Your binding already correctly targets ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader, so no change was needed there.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i list pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

If you need to list pods cluster-wide, we can switch to a ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding.

Steps.message: Verification successful: RBAC role correctly reconfigured.

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- Service endpoints were empty because the Service selector didn’t match the Deployment’s labels.
  - Service selector was app=web
  - Deployment/Pod labels are app=nginx
- Even after fixing the selector, traffic still wouldn’t reach nginx because the container listens on port 8080 (nginx-unprivileged image), while the Service targetPort was 80.
- No NetworkPolicies or Ingress objects in namespace web, and no warning events indicating other issues.

What I changed
- Updated the Service selector to match the Deployment:
  - selector: app=nginx
- Corrected the Service port mapping to the container’s listening port:
  - port: 80 -> targetPort: 8080 (container listens on 8080)
- Verified Endpoints now show the pod and correct port:
  - Endpoints for Service nginx: 10.244.0.34:8080

Current state
- Deployment web/nginx: 1/1 Running
- Service web/nginx: ClusterIP 10.96.241.45, port 80 → targetPort 8080
- Endpoints web/nginx: 10.244.0.34:8080

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n web run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- sh -c "wget -qO- http://nginx && echo OK"
  - Expect to see the default nginx HTML, then OK.
- From your workstation (port-forward):
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - Then browse http://localhost:8080

If you need external access without port-forward
- Option A: Change Service type to NodePort (e.g., nodePort 30080) and reach any node on that port.
- Option B: Create an Ingress mapping host/path to svc/nginx:80 (ensure an Ingress controller is installed).

Summary
Root causes were a mismatched selector (app=web vs app=nginx) and an incorrect targetPort (80 vs actual 8080). Both have been fixed, and the Service now backs to the running nginx pod on the correct port.

Pod deleted successfully
Pod deleted successfully

Steps.message:
namespace/web created
deployment.apps/nginx created
service/nginx created

Diagnosis
- Service endpoints were empty because the Service selector didn’t match the Deployment’s labels.
  - Service selector was app=web
  - Deployment/Pod labels are app=nginx
- Even after fixing the selector, traffic still wouldn’t reach nginx because the container listens on port 8080 (nginx-unprivileged image), while the Service targetPort was 80.
- No NetworkPolicies or Ingress objects in namespace web, and no warning events indicating other issues.

What I changed
- Updated the Service selector to match the Deployment:
  - selector: app=nginx
- Corrected the Service port mapping to the container’s listening port:
  - port: 80 -> targetPort: 8080 (container listens on 8080)
- Verified Endpoints now show the pod and correct port:
  - Endpoints for Service nginx: 10.244.0.34:8080

Current state
- Deployment web/nginx: 1/1 Running
- Service web/nginx: ClusterIP 10.96.241.45, port 80 → targetPort 8080
- Endpoints web/nginx: 10.244.0.34:8080

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n web run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- sh -c "wget -qO- http://nginx && echo OK"
  - Expect to see the default nginx HTML, then OK.
- From your workstation (port-forward):
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - Then browse http://localhost:8080

If you need external access without port-forward
- Option A: Change Service type to NodePort (e.g., nodePort 30080) and reach any node on that port.
- Option B: Create an Ingress mapping host/path to svc/nginx:80 (ensure an Ingress controller is installed).

Summary
Root causes were a mismatched selector (app=web vs app=nginx) and an incorrect targetPort (80 vs actual 8080). Both have been fixed, and the Service now backs to the running nginx pod on the correct port.

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- Service endpoints were empty because the Service selector didn’t match the Deployment’s labels.
  - Service selector was app=web
  - Deployment/Pod labels are app=nginx
- Even after fixing the selector, traffic still wouldn’t reach nginx because the container listens on port 8080 (nginx-unprivileged image), while the Service targetPort was 80.
- No NetworkPolicies or Ingress objects in namespace web, and no warning events indicating other issues.

What I changed
- Updated the Service selector to match the Deployment:
  - selector: app=nginx
- Corrected the Service port mapping to the container’s listening port:
  - port: 80 -> targetPort: 8080 (container listens on 8080)
- Verified Endpoints now show the pod and correct port:
  - Endpoints for Service nginx: 10.244.0.34:8080

Current state
- Deployment web/nginx: 1/1 Running
- Service web/nginx: ClusterIP 10.96.241.45, port 80 → targetPort 8080
- Endpoints web/nginx: 10.244.0.34:8080

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n web run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- sh -c "wget -qO- http://nginx && echo OK"
  - Expect to see the default nginx HTML, then OK.
- From your workstation (port-forward):
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - Then browse http://localhost:8080

If you need external access without port-forward
- Option A: Change Service type to NodePort (e.g., nodePort 30080) and reach any node on that port.
- Option B: Create an Ingress mapping host/path to svc/nginx:80 (ensure an Ingress controller is installed).

Summary
Root causes were a mismatched selector (app=web vs app=nginx) and an incorrect targetPort (80 vs actual 8080). Both have been fixed, and the Service now backs to the running nginx pod on the correct port.

pod "test-connection" deleted from web namespace

results.taskOutput:
Summary of findings
- Root cause: The web-app Deployment had a nodeSelector environment=production-gpu, but no node in the cluster had that label. The scheduler couldn’t place any Pods (FailedScheduling), so the Service had no Endpoints. Your app’s DNS resolved, but the cluster IP had nothing listening, hence “connection refused.”

What I changed to fix it
- Labeled the only node to match the Deployment’s selector:
  - Node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane now has label environment=production-gpu.
- Result:
  - Pods are now running: 2/2 replicas Ready.
  - Service Endpoints are populated with the two Pod IPs on port 80.

Current state (verified)
- Pods (webshop-frontend):
  - web-app-deployment-66f7947765-29kt4: Running (10.244.0.39)
  - web-app-deployment-66f7947765-h94wf: Running (10.244.0.40)
- Service Endpoints:
  - web-app-service -> 10.244.0.39:80, 10.244.0.40:80

Impact
- The Service web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local now has healthy backends. The “connection refused” errors should cease as traffic is routed to the running Pods.

Recommendation
- If the environment=production-gpu selector was unintentional, it’s cleaner to remove or correct it rather than labeling nodes to match:
  - To remove the selector:
    - kubectl -n webshop-frontend patch deploy web-app-deployment --type='json' -p='[{"op":"remove","path":"/spec/template/spec/nodeSelector"}]'
  - Or change it to a label you actually use on nodes (and apply that label cluster-wide as appropriate).
- In multi-node or production clusters, avoid labeling control-plane nodes for workload placement unless that’s intended.

Optional validation
- You can quickly verify in-cluster connectivity:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run --rm -it curl --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- curl -sI http://web-app-service
  - Expect HTTP/1.1 200 OK from nginx.

Let me know if you want me to remove the nodeSelector from the Deployment (and revert the node label) to align with your intended scheduling policy.

arguments.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP

Steps.message:
namespace/webshop-frontend created
service/web-app-service created
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment created
Waiting for resources to be created...
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment condition met
Warning: v1 Endpoints is deprecated in v1.33+; use discovery.k8s.io/v1 EndpointSlice
Setup successful: Service has no endpoints as expected

Summary of findings
- Root cause: The web-app Deployment had a nodeSelector environment=production-gpu, but no node in the cluster had that label. The scheduler couldn’t place any Pods (FailedScheduling), so the Service had no Endpoints. Your app’s DNS resolved, but the cluster IP had nothing listening, hence “connection refused.”

What I changed to fix it
- Labeled the only node to match the Deployment’s selector:
  - Node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane now has label environment=production-gpu.
- Result:
  - Pods are now running: 2/2 replicas Ready.
  - Service Endpoints are populated with the two Pod IPs on port 80.

Current state (verified)
- Pods (webshop-frontend):
  - web-app-deployment-66f7947765-29kt4: Running (10.244.0.39)
  - web-app-deployment-66f7947765-h94wf: Running (10.244.0.40)
- Service Endpoints:
  - web-app-service -> 10.244.0.39:80, 10.244.0.40:80

Impact
- The Service web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local now has healthy backends. The “connection refused” errors should cease as traffic is routed to the running Pods.

Recommendation
- If the environment=production-gpu selector was unintentional, it’s cleaner to remove or correct it rather than labeling nodes to match:
  - To remove the selector:
    - kubectl -n webshop-frontend patch deploy web-app-deployment --type='json' -p='[{"op":"remove","path":"/spec/template/spec/nodeSelector"}]'
  - Or change it to a label you actually use on nodes (and apply that label cluster-wide as appropriate).
- In multi-node or production clusters, avoid labeling control-plane nodes for workload placement unless that’s intended.

Optional validation
- You can quickly verify in-cluster connectivity:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run --rm -it curl --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- curl -sI http://web-app-service
  - Expect HTTP/1.1 200 OK from nginx.

Let me know if you want me to remove the nodeSelector from the Deployment (and revert the node label) to align with your intended scheduling policy.

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          protocol: TCP

outputSteps.content:
Summary of findings
- Root cause: The web-app Deployment had a nodeSelector environment=production-gpu, but no node in the cluster had that label. The scheduler couldn’t place any Pods (FailedScheduling), so the Service had no Endpoints. Your app’s DNS resolved, but the cluster IP had nothing listening, hence “connection refused.”

What I changed to fix it
- Labeled the only node to match the Deployment’s selector:
  - Node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane now has label environment=production-gpu.
- Result:
  - Pods are now running: 2/2 replicas Ready.
  - Service Endpoints are populated with the two Pod IPs on port 80.

Current state (verified)
- Pods (webshop-frontend):
  - web-app-deployment-66f7947765-29kt4: Running (10.244.0.39)
  - web-app-deployment-66f7947765-h94wf: Running (10.244.0.40)
- Service Endpoints:
  - web-app-service -> 10.244.0.39:80, 10.244.0.40:80

Impact
- The Service web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local now has healthy backends. The “connection refused” errors should cease as traffic is routed to the running Pods.

Recommendation
- If the environment=production-gpu selector was unintentional, it’s cleaner to remove or correct it rather than labeling nodes to match:
  - To remove the selector:
    - kubectl -n webshop-frontend patch deploy web-app-deployment --type='json' -p='[{"op":"remove","path":"/spec/template/spec/nodeSelector"}]'
  - Or change it to a label you actually use on nodes (and apply that label cluster-wide as appropriate).
- In multi-node or production clusters, avoid labeling control-plane nodes for workload placement unless that’s intended.

Optional validation
- You can quickly verify in-cluster connectivity:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run --rm -it curl --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- curl -sI http://web-app-service
  - Expect HTTP/1.1 200 OK from nginx.

Let me know if you want me to remove the nodeSelector from the Deployment (and revert the node label) to align with your intended scheduling policy.

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: communication-pod
  namespace: multi-container-logging
  labels:
    app: communication-pod
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: logs-volume
    emptyDir: {}
  containers:
  - name: web-server
    image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
      name: http
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 3
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
  - name: logger
    image: quay.io/prometheus/busybox
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx

Done. The Pod communication-pod is running in the multi-container-logging namespace with:
- web-server: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged, serving on port 8080, readinessProbe httpGet /:8080
- logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox, command: sh -c 'tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log'
- Shared volume logs-volume mounted at /var/log/nginx on both containers

The pod is Ready, gated by the web server’s readiness.

Optional verification:
- Generate a request: kubectl exec -n multi-container-logging communication-pod -c web-server -- wget -qO- http://127.0.0.1:8080/
- See logs in logger: kubectl logs -n multi-container-logging communication-pod -c logger --tail=50

Master/control-plane nodes found: 1
- mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane (Ready)

- Container runtime storage (image/container FS): ~3.14 GiB used (very low)
- Network (since boot): ~899 MiB RX / ~14.8 MiB TX
- Swap: 0 used (3.0 GiB available)
- Resource pressure (PSI): low; no memory or PID pressure, no disk pressure

If you’d like kubectl top to work, I can install/configure metrics-server and then provide ongoing CPU/memory usage via kubectl top nodes.

- Swap: 0 used (3.0 GiB available)
- Resource pressure (PSI): low; no memory or PID pressure, no disk pressure

results.taskOutput:
Done.

- Increased PVC storage-pvc (used by storage-pod) from 1Gi to 3Gi.
- Restarted storage-pod to complete the filesystem resize on the node.
- Verified: PVC capacity is now 3Gi and storage-pod is Running.

You can double-check with:
- kubectl -n resize-pv get pvc storage-pvc
- kubectl -n resize-pv describe pvc storage-pvc
- Optionally inside the pod: df -h /usr/share/nginx/html

outputSteps.content:
Done.

- Increased PVC storage-pvc (used by storage-pod) from 1Gi to 3Gi.
- Restarted storage-pod to complete the filesystem resize on the node.
- Verified: PVC capacity is now 3Gi and storage-pod is Running.

You can double-check with:
- kubectl -n resize-pv get pvc storage-pvc
- kubectl -n resize-pv describe pvc storage-pvc
- Optionally inside the pod: df -h /usr/share/nginx/html

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
  namespace: rollout-test
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 10
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 600
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      containers:

results.taskOutput:
All set. I’ve created the namespaces, RBAC, resource quotas, and network policies to meet your multi-tenant dev cluster requirements. Summary of what’s in place:

1) Namespaces
- Developer: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod
- System namespaces were not modified (no default-deny added there).

2) RBAC
- ServiceAccounts:
  - dev-alice/alice-sa
  - dev-bob/bob-sa
  - dev-charlie/charlie-sa
- Own-namespace full access:
  - Role namespace-owner in each dev namespace granting full rights within that namespace.
  - RoleBinding owner-access in each dev namespace binding:
    - User (alice | bob | charlie)
    - ServiceAccount (alice-sa | bob-sa | charlie-sa)
    to the namespace-owner Role.
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding dev-shared-readers in dev-shared binding:
    - Users: alice, bob, charlie
    - ServiceAccounts: dev-alice/alice-sa, dev-bob/bob-sa, dev-charlie/charlie-sa
  to the ClusterRole view.
- staging and prod:
  - No RoleBindings for developers were created. Only existing cluster-admins (via your cluster’s clusterrolebinding) can access these namespaces.

3) Resource Quotas
- dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie (rq-dev):
  - limits.cpu: 2, limits.memory: 4Gi
  - requests.cpu: 2, requests.memory: 4Gi
  - pods: 10, services: 5
- dev-shared (rq-shared):
  - limits.cpu: 4, limits.memory: 8Gi
  - requests.cpu: 4, requests.memory: 8Gi
  - pods: 20, services: 10
- staging (rq-staging) and prod (rq-prod):
  - limits.cpu: 8, limits.memory: 16Gi
  - requests.cpu: 8, requests.memory: 16Gi
  - pods: 50, services: 20

4) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress and Egress) applied in: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod.
- Intra-namespace allow:
  - allow-same-namespace in each of the above namespaces (permits pod-to-pod within same namespace).
- DNS/system access:
  - allow-dns-egress in each namespace above, permitting UDP/TCP 53 to CoreDNS (kube-system, labeled k8s-app=kube-dns).
- Developer access control:
  - From dev namespaces, only egress to dev-shared is allowed (allow-egress-to-dev-shared in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie).
  - dev-shared allows ingress only from developer namespaces (allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces).
  - Cross-developer traffic is blocked (default-deny + same-namespace-only policies ensure this).
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - No ingress allowed from dev namespaces; only same-namespace traffic and DNS egress allowed.

Notes and assumptions
- The DNS policies target CoreDNS via label k8s-app=kube-dns in kube-system. If your DNS has different labels or runs elsewhere, update the selectors accordingly.
- The “namespace-owner” Role grants full control within each developer namespace (including RBAC within that namespace). If you prefer stricter least-privilege while still giving near-full control, bind ClusterRole edit instead of a wildcard Role; if you want standard “full namespace admin,” bind ClusterRole admin.
- System namespaces (e.g., kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) are intentionally left without default-deny to avoid breaking cluster components.

How to verify
- Namespaces:
  - kubectl get ns
- RBAC:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get sa,role,rolebinding
  - kubectl -n dev-shared get rolebinding dev-shared-readers -o yaml
  - kubectl -n staging get rolebinding
  - kubectl -n prod get rolebinding
- Quotas:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n dev-shared get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n staging get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n prod get resourcequota -o yaml
- NetworkPolicy:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get networkpolicy -o name
  - Repeat for dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod

Optional next steps
- If these developers authenticate via an IdP/OIDC group, create RoleBindings using groups instead of per-user subjects for easier management.
- If pods need to contact the Kubernetes API server directly from inside namespaces under default-deny egress, add an explicit egress rule to the API server address (service or IP) as required by your CNI/network policy behavior.
- Consider adding LimitRanges to set default requests/limits so quotas are effective even when developers omit resources.

If you want, I can adjust the RBAC to use standard ClusterRole admin or edit instead of the custom “namespace-owner” wildcard Role.

Steps.message:
Setting up cluster state for dev environment eval...
Setup completed successfully

All set. I’ve created the namespaces, RBAC, resource quotas, and network policies to meet your multi-tenant dev cluster requirements. Summary of what’s in place:

1) Namespaces
- Developer: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod
- System namespaces were not modified (no default-deny added there).

2) RBAC
- ServiceAccounts:
  - dev-alice/alice-sa
  - dev-bob/bob-sa
  - dev-charlie/charlie-sa
- Own-namespace full access:
  - Role namespace-owner in each dev namespace granting full rights within that namespace.
  - RoleBinding owner-access in each dev namespace binding:
    - User (alice | bob | charlie)
    - ServiceAccount (alice-sa | bob-sa | charlie-sa)
    to the namespace-owner Role.
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding dev-shared-readers in dev-shared binding:
    - Users: alice, bob, charlie
    - ServiceAccounts: dev-alice/alice-sa, dev-bob/bob-sa, dev-charlie/charlie-sa
  to the ClusterRole view.
- staging and prod:
  - No RoleBindings for developers were created. Only existing cluster-admins (via your cluster’s clusterrolebinding) can access these namespaces.

3) Resource Quotas
- dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie (rq-dev):
  - limits.cpu: 2, limits.memory: 4Gi
  - requests.cpu: 2, requests.memory: 4Gi
  - pods: 10, services: 5
- dev-shared (rq-shared):
  - limits.cpu: 4, limits.memory: 8Gi
  - requests.cpu: 4, requests.memory: 8Gi
  - pods: 20, services: 10
- staging (rq-staging) and prod (rq-prod):
  - limits.cpu: 8, limits.memory: 16Gi
  - requests.cpu: 8, requests.memory: 16Gi
  - pods: 50, services: 20

4) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress and Egress) applied in: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod.
- Intra-namespace allow:
  - allow-same-namespace in each of the above namespaces (permits pod-to-pod within same namespace).
- DNS/system access:
  - allow-dns-egress in each namespace above, permitting UDP/TCP 53 to CoreDNS (kube-system, labeled k8s-app=kube-dns).
- Developer access control:
  - From dev namespaces, only egress to dev-shared is allowed (allow-egress-to-dev-shared in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie).
  - dev-shared allows ingress only from developer namespaces (allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces).
  - Cross-developer traffic is blocked (default-deny + same-namespace-only policies ensure this).
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - No ingress allowed from dev namespaces; only same-namespace traffic and DNS egress allowed.

Notes and assumptions
- The DNS policies target CoreDNS via label k8s-app=kube-dns in kube-system. If your DNS has different labels or runs elsewhere, update the selectors accordingly.
- The “namespace-owner” Role grants full control within each developer namespace (including RBAC within that namespace). If you prefer stricter least-privilege while still giving near-full control, bind ClusterRole edit instead of a wildcard Role; if you want standard “full namespace admin,” bind ClusterRole admin.
- System namespaces (e.g., kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) are intentionally left without default-deny to avoid breaking cluster components.

How to verify
- Namespaces:
  - kubectl get ns
- RBAC:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get sa,role,rolebinding
  - kubectl -n dev-shared get rolebinding dev-shared-readers -o yaml
  - kubectl -n staging get rolebinding
  - kubectl -n prod get rolebinding
- Quotas:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n dev-shared get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n staging get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n prod get resourcequota -o yaml
- NetworkPolicy:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get networkpolicy -o name
  - Repeat for dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod

Optional next steps
- If these developers authenticate via an IdP/OIDC group, create RoleBindings using groups instead of per-user subjects for easier management.
- If pods need to contact the Kubernetes API server directly from inside namespaces under default-deny egress, add an explicit egress rule to the API server address (service or IP) as required by your CNI/network policy behavior.
- Consider adding LimitRanges to set default requests/limits so quotas are effective even when developers omit resources.

If you want, I can adjust the RBAC to use standard ClusterRole admin or edit instead of the custom “namespace-owner” wildcard Role.

outputSteps.content:
All set. I’ve created the namespaces, RBAC, resource quotas, and network policies to meet your multi-tenant dev cluster requirements. Summary of what’s in place:

1) Namespaces
- Developer: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod
- System namespaces were not modified (no default-deny added there).

2) RBAC
- ServiceAccounts:
  - dev-alice/alice-sa
  - dev-bob/bob-sa
  - dev-charlie/charlie-sa
- Own-namespace full access:
  - Role namespace-owner in each dev namespace granting full rights within that namespace.
  - RoleBinding owner-access in each dev namespace binding:
    - User (alice | bob | charlie)
    - ServiceAccount (alice-sa | bob-sa | charlie-sa)
    to the namespace-owner Role.
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding dev-shared-readers in dev-shared binding:
    - Users: alice, bob, charlie
    - ServiceAccounts: dev-alice/alice-sa, dev-bob/bob-sa, dev-charlie/charlie-sa
  to the ClusterRole view.
- staging and prod:
  - No RoleBindings for developers were created. Only existing cluster-admins (via your cluster’s clusterrolebinding) can access these namespaces.

3) Resource Quotas
- dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie (rq-dev):
  - limits.cpu: 2, limits.memory: 4Gi
  - requests.cpu: 2, requests.memory: 4Gi
  - pods: 10, services: 5
- dev-shared (rq-shared):
  - limits.cpu: 4, limits.memory: 8Gi
  - requests.cpu: 4, requests.memory: 8Gi
  - pods: 20, services: 10
- staging (rq-staging) and prod (rq-prod):
  - limits.cpu: 8, limits.memory: 16Gi
  - requests.cpu: 8, requests.memory: 16Gi
  - pods: 50, services: 20

4) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress and Egress) applied in: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod.
- Intra-namespace allow:
  - allow-same-namespace in each of the above namespaces (permits pod-to-pod within same namespace).
- DNS/system access:
  - allow-dns-egress in each namespace above, permitting UDP/TCP 53 to CoreDNS (kube-system, labeled k8s-app=kube-dns).
- Developer access control:
  - From dev namespaces, only egress to dev-shared is allowed (allow-egress-to-dev-shared in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie).
  - dev-shared allows ingress only from developer namespaces (allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces).
  - Cross-developer traffic is blocked (default-deny + same-namespace-only policies ensure this).
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - No ingress allowed from dev namespaces; only same-namespace traffic and DNS egress allowed.

Notes and assumptions
- The DNS policies target CoreDNS via label k8s-app=kube-dns in kube-system. If your DNS has different labels or runs elsewhere, update the selectors accordingly.
- The “namespace-owner” Role grants full control within each developer namespace (including RBAC within that namespace). If you prefer stricter least-privilege while still giving near-full control, bind ClusterRole edit instead of a wildcard Role; if you want standard “full namespace admin,” bind ClusterRole admin.
- System namespaces (e.g., kube-system, kube-public, kube-node-lease) are intentionally left without default-deny to avoid breaking cluster components.

How to verify
- Namespaces:
  - kubectl get ns
- RBAC:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get sa,role,rolebinding
  - kubectl -n dev-shared get rolebinding dev-shared-readers -o yaml
  - kubectl -n staging get rolebinding
  - kubectl -n prod get rolebinding
- Quotas:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n dev-shared get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n staging get resourcequota -o yaml
  - kubectl -n prod get resourcequota -o yaml
- NetworkPolicy:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice get networkpolicy -o name
  - Repeat for dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod

Optional next steps
- If these developers authenticate via an IdP/OIDC group, create RoleBindings using groups instead of per-user subjects for easier management.
- If pods need to contact the Kubernetes API server directly from inside namespaces under default-deny egress, add an explicit egress rule to the API server address (service or IP) as required by your CNI/network policy behavior.
- Consider adding LimitRanges to set default requests/limits so quotas are effective even when developers omit resources.

If you want, I can adjust the RBAC to use standard ClusterRole admin or edit instead of the custom “namespace-owner” wildcard Role.

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: db
  namespace: statefulset-test
spec:
  serviceName: db
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: db
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: db
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: db
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sh", "-c", "test -f /data/test || (mkdir -p /data && echo -n initial_data > /data/test); tail -f /dev/null"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 10
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 1Gi

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I created
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: statefulset-test/db
- StatefulSet: statefulset-test/db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox:1.36
  - Mount: /data from per-pod PVC
  - VolumeClaimTemplate: 1Gi, RWO
  - Init logic: creates /data/test with contents “initial_data” if it doesn’t exist

Verification
- db-0:/data/test content: initial_data

Scaled down
- StatefulSet db scaled to 1 replica
- Current Pods: db-0 Running
- PVCs: data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2 remain Bound (retained); data on db-1/db-2 will persist and be reused if scaled up again.

If you want me to clean up the extra PVCs left by the scaled-down replicas, say so and I’ll remove them.

Old pods are deleted

StatefulSet is running with 1 replicas
